codeReview.summary: The v2 lexer is a faithful port of v1's conf/lex.go with targeted additions for v2 needs: comment style tracking, key separator tracking, integer overflow detection, and exported token types. The implementation covers all 10 feature requirements: all scalar types, key separators, escape sequences, comments, structural tokens, special cases (IP/bcrypt/JSON), value terminators, CRLF support, negative floats, and error reporting. The code structure closely mirrors v1, using the same channel-based state machine (Rob Pike pattern), identical state function names and logic, and matching token emission. Test coverage is comprehensive with 82 lex tests covering every expected behavior, cross-validation against v1 expected output, overflow boundary tests, and CRLF tests for 18 value type categories.

issues.description: checkIntegerOverflow strips suffix bytes one at a time from the end, checking for ASCII digits or '-'. This works for all current suffix patterns (k/K/m/M/g/G/t/T/p/P/e/E/b/B/i/I) but would silently accept malformed numeric strings where non-suffix characters are embedded before the suffix (e.g., 'x' before 'k'). In practice this cannot happen because the lexer state machine only calls checkIntegerOverflow after validating the digit+suffix structure via lexConvenientNumber/lexNegNumber/lexNumberOrDateOrStringOrIP, so this is safe but worth noting for future maintenance.

TestLexCRLFAllValueTypes covers 18 value type categories but does not include block strings (parenthesized multiline content). Block strings use a hardcoded '\n' check (not isNL) in the lexBlock state function for detecting the closing ')' on a bare line, which means CRLF-only line endings before ')' would not terminate the block correctly. However, this matches v1 behavior exactly (v1 has the same limitation in lexBlock), so it is not a v2 regression. A test documenting this v1-inherited limitation could be valuable.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker's skill feedback suggests that the TDD 'red' phase guidance in the go-library-worker skill could be adapted for features where substantial code already exists from a prior commit. The current skill says 'Write failing tests BEFORE implementation' but when a prior worker session already wrote the bulk of the code (82 tests from prior commit ea46f0af8), the second session focused on gap-filling (overflow detection, CRLF comprehensiveness). The skill could acknowledge this incremental TDD pattern.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff skillFeedback.suggestedChanges: 'The skill could mention that for features where substantial code already exists from a prior commit, the TDD red phase should focus on the gaps/additions rather than re-testing everything from scratch.' Worker session 7abd516d was the second session for this feature (first was e542830a).

sharedStateObservations.observation: Block strings (parenthesized multiline content) have a CRLF limitation inherited from v1: the lexBlock function checks specifically for '\n' character (not isNL which also accepts '\r') when detecting the closing ')' on a bare line. This means block strings in files with CRLF-only line endings (\r\n before ')') may not terminate correctly. This is a known v1 behavior that v2 faithfully reproduces, but it could be documented in AGENTS.md or the library as a known limitation for future reference.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: conf/v2/lex.go lexBlock function uses 'if lx.next() != '\n'' at line ~959 and 'case '\n', eof:' at line ~968, matching conf/lex.go's identical logic. The isNL function (which handles \r) is not used here.

summary: The v2 lexer passes review. It is a faithful, well-tested port of v1 with clean additions for v2 needs (comment style tracking, key separator tracking, integer overflow detection). All 10 feature requirements from the description are implemented and tested. The 82 lex tests provide comprehensive coverage including cross-validation against v1 expected output. Two non-blocking observations: (1) CRLF test coverage doesn't include block strings, matching a v1 limitation; (2) checkIntegerOverflow's suffix stripping is safe due to lexer state machine constraints but could be more explicit. No blocking issues found.
